summary: chai-plugin impersonates chai and executes remote code on require()
details: Package presents itself as a plugin for the popular `chai` assertion library (matching description, contributor list, version scheme, and README). On `require('chai-plugin')`, `lib/chai.js` invokes `validate_assert({ source: 'require-load' })`, which spawns a detached Node child process (`spawn('node', [lib/chai/utils/assertion.js,...], { detached: true, stdio: 'ignore' })` and `child.unref()`) so execution continues silently after the parent returns. The child file `lib/chai/utils/assertion.js` is heavily obfuscated (obfuscator.io-style string-array / hex-name encoding), uses the `http` module to fetch a remote URL with an embedded token, and passes the response body directly to `new Function('require', body)(require)` — evaluating attacker-controlled JavaScript with full access to `require`. This is a runtime stage-2 dropper: whatever the remote endpoint returns will execute on the installer's machine with the privileges of the Node process, with no constraint on payload (credential theft, persistence, lateral movement, etc.). The combination of typosquat-style impersonation of `chai`, detached/silent child process, obfuscation, and dynamic evaluation of a network response is an unambiguous supply-chain attack.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
